ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fish ball entry; no fried-specific variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers processed fish balls; fried preparation variant not separately coded. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried fish ball as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fried fish balls is approximately 4 pieces weighing around 60 g, commonly eaten as a snack or side dish in Singapore hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Fish Ball — Singapore HPB Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for fried fish ball (F03040010). All core macros and micronutrients reflect the HPB record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on processed fish product with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fried fish ball. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on analogous processed fish/seafood products with minimal carbohydrate (1.4 g/100g). Glycemic load per 60 g serving = (40 × 1.4 × 0.60) / 100 = 0.34, rounded to 0.3. This is consistent with low-carbohydrate processed fish products in the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 650 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold for hypertension-sensitive foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit fried fish ball intake; sodium at 650 mg/100 g significantly contributes to daily sodium load, and dipping sauces (chilli, soy) will further elevate intake — choose steamed or boiled fish balls and avoid high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (1.4 g/100 g) and low estimated GI (~40) with high protein (14.2 g/100 g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried fish balls are suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to minimal carbohydrate and high protein content; however, deep-frying adds fat calories and sweet dipping sauces should be avoided to prevent glycaemic excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (650 mg/100 g) and deep-fried preparation raise cardiovascular risk despite low total fat (2.7 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content (650 mg/100 g) is the primary concern as it contributes to blood pressure elevation; deep-frying also introduces oxidised lipids — limit portion size, avoid sodium-rich condiments, and prefer boiled or steamed preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; sodium 650 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid fried fish balls as deep-frying generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation, and the high sodium content (650 mg/100 g) worsens RA-related tissue swelling — choose boiled or steamed fish balls instead.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried fish ball (炸鱼丸) is a popular processed seafood snack widely consumed in Singapore and across Chinese-influenced Southeast Asian communities. It is made from scraped or minced white fish meat — typically ikan parang (wolf herring) or other lean fish — blended with salt, starch, and water to form a smooth, springy paste that is shaped into round balls and deep-fried until golden. The exterior is lightly crisp while the interior remains bouncy and firm, with a savoury, mildly fishy flavour. Fried fish balls are a staple at Singapore hawker centres, often served on skewers with chilli sauce or as a component in fish ball noodle soup. Nutritionally, they are high in protein (14.2 g/100 g) and low in carbohydrates (1.4 g/100 g), making them suitable for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns. However, the sodium content is high at 650 mg/100 g due to salt used in processing, which is a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The deep-fried preparation also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs). Dietary fibre is negligible (0.0 g/100 g confirmed per HPB). Energy density is relatively low at 87 kcal/100 g.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F03040010) at confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary regional approved source for Singapore foods. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence for GI component only) as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific product.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore database; fish paste products contain negligible dietary fibre by formulation. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific fried fish ball entry are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F03040010) as of April 2026. GI value of 40 is an analogous estimate based on processed fish products with low carbohydrate content from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for fried fish ball was found in approved sources. Sodium at 650 mg/100 g exceeds the 600 mg/100 g hypertension hard gate and the 400 mg/100 g rheumatoid arthritis threshold.
